---
title: "Project 2.1 — Approximate Bayesian Inference · INSTRUCTOR SOLUTION"
subtitle: "From Inference to Discovery · Gate A · 1 of 2"
author: "Reference solution (instructor-only)"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.callout-important}
## Instructor-only reference solution
Do **not** distribute. Implementations in `solutions/_reference.py`; see
`solutions/VALIDATION.md`.
:::

```{python}
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "..")))
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from info521 import data, plotting, checks
import _reference as ref

plotting.use_course_style()
ds = data.load_clinical()
X_raw, feat = ds["X"], ds["features"]
labels, rule = data.hypertension(ds)
print(f"{len(labels)} patients · features {feat} · {rule} · prev {labels.mean():.3f}")
```

## 0 · Split and standardize (no leakage)

```{python}
Xtr_r, Xte_r, ytr, yte = ref.train_test_split(X_raw, labels, test_frac=0.25, seed=521)
mu, sd = ref.standardize_fit(Xtr_r)                 # TRAIN-only statistics
Xtr = ref.add_intercept(ref.standardize_apply(Xtr_r, mu, sd))
Xte = ref.add_intercept(ref.standardize_apply(Xte_r, mu, sd))
ytr = ytr.astype(float); yte = yte.astype(float)
tau2 = 100.0                                        # prior variance (intercept unpenalized)
print("train/test:", Xtr.shape, Xte.shape)
```

## 1 · The model, and why it is non-conjugate

Prior $\mathbf w\sim\mathcal N(\mathbf 0,\tau^2\mathbf I)$ (intercept left
unpenalized); likelihood $y_n\sim\text{Bernoulli}(\sigma(\mathbf w^\top\mathbf x_n))$.
The product of a Gaussian and a sigmoid-Bernoulli is **not** the kernel of any
standard density — there is no closed-form posterior, unlike the Gaussian×Gaussian
conjugacy of 1.3. So we approximate it three ways.

## 2 · Log-posterior, gradient, Hessian — verify the gradient first

```{python}
f  = lambda w: ref.neg_log_posterior(w, Xtr, ytr, tau2)
gf = lambda w: ref.grad_neg_log_posterior(w, Xtr, ytr, tau2)
w0 = np.full(Xtr.shape[1], 0.1)
checks.expect_gradient(f, gf, w0, name="neg-log-posterior gradient")
```

## 3 · Newton–Raphson (IRLS) to the MAP

```{python}
w_map, hist = ref.fit_map_newton(Xtr, ytr, tau2)
print(f"Newton iterations to converge: {len(hist)}")
print("grad-norm per iter:", [f"{h:.2e}" for h in hist])
print("MAP weights:", dict(zip(['1']+feat, np.round(w_map, 3))))
```

Newton converges in a handful of steps (quadratic convergence) — the gradient norm
collapses to ~1e-13.

## 4 · Laplace approximation

```{python}
_, cov = ref.laplace_posterior(Xtr, ytr, tau2, w_map)
lap_sd = np.sqrt(np.diag(cov))
print("Laplace posterior SDs:", dict(zip(['1']+feat, np.round(lap_sd, 3))))
```

## 5 · Metropolis sampler

```{python}
chain, acc = ref.metropolis(Xtr, ytr, tau2, w_map, n_samples=15000,
                            proposal_sd=0.02, burn_in=3000, seed=1)
print(f"acceptance rate (proposal_sd=0.02): {acc:.3f}  ·  kept {len(chain)} samples")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 3.2))
for j, name in [(1, feat[0]), (3, feat[2])]:   # age, waist coefficients
    axes[0].plot(chain[:, j], lw=0.5, label=name)
axes[0].set_title("traces (fuzzy caterpillar = good mixing)")
axes[0].set_xlabel("iteration"); axes[0].legend()
axes[1].hist(chain[:, 1], bins=40, color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["skyblue"])
axes[1].set_title(f"posterior of the '{feat[0]}' coefficient")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

print("Metropolis post mean:", dict(zip(['1']+feat, np.round(chain.mean(0), 3))))
print("Metropolis post SD  :", dict(zip(['1']+feat, np.round(chain.std(0), 3))))
```

## 6 · Three treatments, one posterior

```{python}
j = 1  # the 'age' coefficient
lo, hi = chain[:, j].min(), chain[:, j].max()
xs = np.linspace(lo, hi, 200)
gauss = np.exp(-0.5*((xs - w_map[j])/lap_sd[j])**2) / (lap_sd[j]*np.sqrt(2*np.pi))

ax = plt.gca()
ax.hist(chain[:, j], bins=40, density=True, alpha=0.5,
        color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["skyblue"], label="Metropolis samples")
ax.plot(xs, gauss, color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], lw=2, label="Laplace Gaussian")
ax.axvline(w_map[j], color="black", ls="--", label="MAP point")
ax.set_xlabel(f"'{feat[0]}' coefficient"); ax.legend(); plt.show()

print(f"MAP={w_map[j]:.3f} | Laplace mean={w_map[j]:.3f} sd={lap_sd[j]:.3f} | "
      f"Metropolis mean={chain[:,j].mean():.3f} sd={chain[:,j].std():.3f}")
```

**The parallel (≈180 words).** The three treatments are precisely Project 1's three
lenses, transplanted past the conjugacy break. **MAP** is the *point* estimate — the
mode of the posterior, the direct analogue of OLS/MLE's single $\hat{\mathbf w}$ in
1.1. **Laplace** wraps that point in a *Gaussian* read off the Hessian curvature —
the analogue of 1.2's MLE-plus-error-bars, an analytic uncertainty around the
estimate. **Metropolis** draws from the *true* posterior — the analogue of 1.3's full
Bayesian distribution. What is genuinely new is that here the posterior is
*approximated*, not exact: in 1.3 conjugacy handed us the Gaussian for free; here we
either settle for a Gaussian approximation (Laplace) or pay for samples (Metropolis).
On this cohort the three agree tightly — the MAP, the Laplace mean, and the
Metropolis mean coincide to three decimals, and the Laplace and Metropolis SDs match
to ~5% — because $N\approx3800$ makes the posterior very nearly Gaussian. That
agreement is itself the lesson: with abundant data the cheap approximation is
excellent; the machinery earns its keep when data is scarce or the posterior is
skewed.

---

## Play artifact — prior variance & proposal scale

```{python}
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))

# (1) prior variance: weights shrink as tau^2 shrinks
taus = np.logspace(-2, 3, 12)
norms = []
for t2 in taus:
    wm, _ = ref.fit_map_newton(Xtr, ytr, t2)
    norms.append(np.linalg.norm(wm[1:]))     # exclude intercept
axL.semilogx(taus, norms, marker="o", color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"])
axL.set_xlabel("prior variance τ²"); axL.set_ylabel("‖MAP weights‖ (excl. intercept)")
axL.set_title("tight prior ⇒ shrinkage")

# (2) proposal scale: acceptance rate
scales = [0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2]
accs = []
for s in scales:
    _, a = ref.metropolis(Xtr, ytr, tau2, w_map, n_samples=4000,
                          proposal_sd=s, burn_in=1000, seed=3)
    accs.append(a)
axR.semilogx(scales, accs, marker="s", color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["blue"])
axR.axhspan(0.2, 0.5, alpha=0.15, color="green")
axR.set_xlabel("proposal SD"); axR.set_ylabel("acceptance rate")
axR.set_title("healthy band ≈ 0.2–0.5 (shaded)")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

print("τ² → ‖w‖:", [f"{t:.2g}:{n:.2f}" for t, n in zip(taus[::3], norms[::3])])
print("scale → acc:", [f"{s}:{a:.2f}" for s, a in zip(scales, accs)])
```

**Interpretation (≈200 words).** *Prior variance* behaves exactly as in 1.3: a tight
prior (small $\tau^2$) pulls the coefficient norm toward zero — Bayesian shrinkage —
while a vague prior lets the likelihood dominate and the norm plateaus at the
unregularized MLE scale. *Proposal scale* shows the classic Metropolis tuning
tradeoff. Too small (0.005) and almost every proposal is accepted (~0.85) but each
step is tiny, so the chain crawls and explores slowly. Too large (≥0.1) and nearly
every proposal lands in a low-probability region and is rejected (~0.01), so the
chain sticks in place. The healthy zone is the shaded 0.2–0.5 band, reached here
around proposal_sd ≈ 0.02; that is why the §5 run used it. The two knobs answer
different questions — $\tau^2$ shapes *what posterior you are sampling*, the proposal
scale shapes *how efficiently you sample it* — and an honest Bayesian workflow has to
get both right before trusting any posterior summary.

::: {.callout-note}
## Gate
Gated by **Checkpoint 4 (Newton–Raphson update)**.
:::

## Looking ahead

2.2 keeps this split and these features but swaps the probabilistic classifier for a
margin-based SVM.
